coredata.dc:title: Bridging biological and food monitoring: A colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode sensor based on N-doped carbon dots for detection of pH and histamine
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xiaoran
Wang, Jing
Hasan, Elias
Sun, Xincheng
Asif, Muhammad
Aziz, Ayesha
Lu, Wenjing
Dong, Chuan
Shuang, Shaomin
coredata.dc:description: Rapid and sensitive monitoring of pH and histamine is crucial for bridging biological and food systems and identifying corresponding abnormal situations. Herein, N-doped carbon dots (CDs) are fabricated by a hydrothermal method employing dipicolinic acid and o-phenylenediamine as precursors. The CDs exhibit colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode responses to track pH and histamine variations in living cells and food freshness, respectively. The aggregation-induced emission enhancement and intramolecular charge transfer result in a decrease in absorbance and an increase in fluorescence, which become readily apparent as the pH changes from acidic to neutral. This property enables precise differentiation between normal and cancerous cells. Furthermore, given the intrinsic basicity of histamine, pH-responsive CDs are advantageous for additional colorimetric and fluorescent monitoring of histamine in food freshness, achieving linearities of 25–1000 µM and 30–1000 µM, respectively, which are broader than those of alternative nanoprobes. Interestingly, the smartphone-integrated sensing platform can portably and visually evaluate pH and histamine changes due to sensitive color changes. Therefore, the sensor not only establishes a dynamic connection between pH and histamine for the purposes of biological and food monitoring, but also presents a novel approach for developing a multifunctional biosensor that can accomplish environmental monitoring and biosensing simultaneously.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Dual-modal sensing
Smartphone-integrated sensor
Intracellular pH monitoring
Food freshness

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Absorbance and emission properties of CDs
Colorimetric and fluorescence sensing of pH
Colorimetric and fluorescence sensing of histamine
Smartphone imaging detection of pH and histamine
The stability of CDs for pH and histamine sensing
Cell imaging of pH and histamine
Practicability of histamine detection in samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22274090) and Shanxi Province Graduate Student Education Innovation Project (2023KY022).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of CDs and their insets are HRTEM image and size distribution histograms of as-prepared CDs, respectively. (b) FTIR spectrum of CDs. (c) XPS wide-scan spectrum, (d) C 1 s XPS spectrum, (e) N 1 s XPS spectrum, and O1s XPS spectrum of CDs.

Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs in glycerol–water mixtures (a) and DMF–water mixtures (c). Changes in the relative PL intensity (I/I0) with the composition of glycerol–water mixtures (b) and DMF–water mixtures (d). Insets: fluorescent photos of the aqueous mixtures taken under a 365 nm UV lamp.

Changes in UV–vis absorption spectra with the pH from 3.0 to 5.6 (a) and their corresponding linearity of response to pH (b). Changes in fluorescence spectra with the pH from 3.2 to 6.8 (c) and their corresponding linearity of response to pH (d).

(a) The repeated cycles of absorbance spectra from pH 3.0 to pH 5.6. (b) The repeated cycles of fluorescence spectra from pH 3.2 to pH 6.8. (c) The selectivity of relative absorbance and (d) relative fluorescence values to different potential interfering substances at different pHs. (Interfering substances: Blank, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Zn2+,Cd2+, Cl-, SO4 2-, SO3 2-, NO-, Ac-, H2O2, ClO-, Cys, GSH, Hcy, Asp, Glu, AA, Lyr, Leu, Tyr, Phe, DA).
(a) Cyclic voltammogram and (b) Energy levels of CDs at pH 3.0 and 7.0. (c)Working mechanism of CDs for pH sensing.

Changes in UV–vis absorption spectra (a) and fluorescence spectra (d) with increasing histamine. Their corresponding linearities are in UV–vis absorption spectra (b) and fluorescence spectra (e). The absorbance (c) and fluorescence (f) response of CDs toward histamine and interfering substances (10 mM).

The imaging and calibration curves of CDs for pH and histamine monitoring captured by smartphone (OPPO Reno SE 5 G). Diverse pHs under (a) sunlight and (b) UV lamps. Various histamine concentrations under (c) sunlight and (d) UV lamps.
Cell images of CDs at various pHs in HeLa cells.
Assaying fish specimens freshness at four various temperatures and different time by CDs under sunlight (a) and UV lamps (b).
Scheme diagram of synthesis of carbon dots.

head.ce:title: Bridging biological and food monitoring: A colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode sensor based on N-doped carbon dots for detection of pH and histamine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rapid and sensitive monitoring of pH and histamine is crucial for bridging biological and food systems and identifying corresponding abnormal situations. Herein, N-doped carbon dots (CDs) are fabricated by a hydrothermal method employing dipicolinic acid and o-phenylenediamine as precursors. The CDs exhibit colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode responses to track pH and histamine variations in living cells and food freshness, respectively. The aggregation-induced emission enhancement and intramolecular charge transfer result in a decrease in absorbance and an increase in fluorescence, which become readily apparent as the pH changes from acidic to neutral. This property enables precise differentiation between normal and cancerous cells. Furthermore, given the intrinsic basicity of histamine, pH-responsive CDs are advantageous for additional colorimetric and fluorescent monitoring of histamine in food freshness, achieving linearities of 25–1000 µM and 30–1000 µM, respectively, which are broader than those of alternative nanoprobes. Interestingly, the smartphone-integrated sensing platform can portably and visually evaluate pH and histamine changes due to sensitive color changes. Therefore, the sensor not only establishes a dynamic connection between pH and histamine for the purposes of biological and food monitoring, but also presents a novel approach for developing a multifunctional biosensor that can accomplish environmental monitoring and biosensing simultaneously.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Text: A colorimetric and fluorescent CDs-based sensor has been fabricates by employing dipicolinic acid and o-phenylenediamine as precursors, which can enable sensitively tracking pH and histamine in cell imaging and food freshness. Intriguingly, by integrating smartphone, CDs can portably and visually evaluate pH and histamine changes due to sensitive color changes. The nano-sensor not only establishes a dynamic connection between pH and histamine for the purposes of biological and food monitoring with multifunctional modes among colorimetry, fluorescence and smartphone-integrated portable assay, but also reveals a new guideline for designing multifunctional biosensor for bridging biosensing and environmental application concurrently.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the modern era, pH values are essential physical and chemical factors that hold significant importance in diverse areas, including chemical reactions, biological activities, environmental regulation, and food safety [1–4]. Moreover, intracellular pH significantly impacts the modulation of cell and organism functions and activities, primarily including signal transduction, proliferation, and apoptosis [5]. Even small changes in acidity levels, measured by pH values, can disrupt normal metabolic processes, enhance the occurrence and metastasis of tumors, and thus lead to several serious illnesses [6,7]. Therefore, real-time and accurate detection of intracellular pH fluctuations is of great importance and provides pivotal assistance for early diagnosis and cancer therapy.

Histamine is a nitrogen-containing compound formed via histidine under the action of decarboxylase, possessing a proper concentration of 213.5 ± 178.9 nmol/L in human blood and employing as an ideal mediator in various physiological and pathological processes [8]. However, abnormal levels of histamine bind to G protein-coupled histamine receptors and wreak allergic reactions in the body, leading to numerous symptoms such as skin irritation, headache and vomiting. [9,10]. Therefore, the strict control over the threshold histamine levels for human intake is necessary. In daily life, high amounts of histamine are commonly generated by the enzymatic decarboxylation of histidine in spoiled microorganisms such as fishes, shrimps and scallops due to their inappropriate transportation and storage practices [11,12]. Consequently, reliable and effective histamine evaluation can serve the dual purpose of indicating food quality and safeguarding human health. To date, the majority of pH-responsive analyses have been utilized to monitor histamine, considering the inherent basicity of the system, which induces pH variations in the solution after histamine addition [13,14]. Therefore, searching and constructing pH-responsive probes can achieve sensitive detection of pH and histamine.

So far, numerous analyses have been exploited for detecting pH or histamine, including enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays, nuclear magnetic resonance technology, and gas chromatography, among others [15–18]. These methods possess high accuracy and good repeatability. However, complex chemical procedures and expensive instruments make it difficult to satisfy the demands for real-time and sensitive pH and histamine sensing. In recent years, colorimetric and fluorescent methods have been widely considered as effective strategies for detecting pH and histamine due to their simple operation and fast response [13,19]. For instance, Alamdari et al. constructed a pH-sensitive colorimetric sensing system based on Ixiolirion-tataricum anthocyanins to track histamine with absorbance and color changes [20]. Liao et al. synthesized a ratiometric fluorescence sensor with nanozyme merits for sensitively determining histamine [21]. Regrettably, when considering their practical implementation, colorimetric or fluorescent methods that rely on single-signal changes are invariably limited by inherent flaws, such as excessive instrument dependence, inadequate adaptability, and poor result credibility [22,23]. As a consequence, the desired outcomes are impeded. To overcome these obstacles, simultaneous colorimetric and fluorescent dual-signal response technologies have been proposed to integrate all the benefits of a single analysis and achieve sensitive monitoring of complex analytes, which can yield more convincing experimental results [24]. Since the first breakthrough [25] when colorimetric and fluorescent method could simultaneously identify and quantify analytes in the 1990 s, an obvious increase in dual-mode measurements of pH or histamine has been witnessed. In 2019, Tang et al. reported a pH-sensitive AIE nanoprobe for the colorimetric and fluorescent sensing of pH and biogenic amine based on a simple method, offering a promising strategy for achieving both bioimaging and food spoilage determination [12]. However, to date, no sensing platform possesses fluorescent and colorimetric detection capabilities for bridging biological and food imaging to enable the simultaneous analysis of pH and histamine. Therefore, the rational design of materials with fluorescent and colorimetric modes to simultaneously detect pH and histamine is highly desired.

Carbon dots (CDs), emerging fluorescent nanomaterials, have significantly contributed to energy conversion, photocatalysis, biosensors and bioimaging owing to their simple synthesis, biocompatibility, easy functionalization, and ultra-high photostability [26,27]. So far, noteworthy efforts have been dedicated to designing CDs-based nano-sensors for the efficient evaluation of biomarkers and harmful environmental substances [28]. Peng et al. designed NIR-emitting CDs to identify bacterial species and viability through interactions between the surface of Cy7-CDs and biomolecules within bacteria [29]. Liu et al. developed novel CDs for turn-on fluorescence to track endogenous and exogenous ONOO- [30]. Jiao et al. reported red fluorescent CDs for visually detecting metabisulfite and pH via charge transfer and protonation/deprotonation of nitrogen atom [31]. Nonetheless, the single-fluorescence-signal variations of sensing systems need further improvement, potentially causing unnecessary signal fluctuations in the instrumental parameters/environment [32]. Consequently, integration with other meaningful signals is very effective for increasing credibility in complex systems. Colorimetric signals have attracted considerable attentions due to the merits of simple operation, naked-eye readout and in-situ availability [33]. Therefore, designing and constructing dual-responsive colorimetric and fluorescent CDs-based sensing platforms is imperative.

Herein, we fabricate a colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode CDs, that can function as a multifunctional sensor for rapidly and sensitively determining pH and histamine in vivo and in vitro. The novel sensor with excellent optical characteristics is developed by exploiting an easy synthetic process and cheap raw materials, including dipicolinic acid and o-phenylenediamine. The designed CDs achieve sensitive and accurate determination of pH and histamine in both colorimetric and fluorescent assays. This not only exhibits an exceptional biological sensing application for screening normal and cancer cells by monitoring abnormal intracellular pH and histamine levels, but also visually evaluates food freshness by detecting aberrant histamine variations, demonstrating an instant response (120 s) and ultrahigh sensitivity. Additionally, by integrating a smartphone and color-recognizing APP, the CDs-based sensor demonstrates promising visual and portable detection of pH and histamine via distinctive color variations in the presence of sunlight and UV lamps. To endow CDs with more intelligent and multifunctional capabilities, the correlation between colorimetry and fluorescence, cell imaging, food freshness, and color discrimination has been established in comparison to alternative nanoprobes.

Dipicolinic acid (DPA) and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) are used as precursors for the preparation of CDs via a one-pot solvothermal method combined with a simple purification process ( Scheme 1). The morphology and structural characteristics of CDs are examined using Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Dynamic light scattering (DLS), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 1a, CDs exhibit a uniform spherical property with a mean particle size of 1.88 ± 0.31 nm and the lattice spacing of 0.122 nm is displayed by the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) spectrum in Fig. S1 also verifies the hydrodynamic sizes of CDs are about 1.931 nm (PDI: 0.236), which is consistent with TEM. The chemical structure and elemental distribution of the as-prepared CDs are further analyzed using FT-IR and XPS photoelectron spectroscopy. It is clearly seen in Fig. 1b that absorption bands at 3407 cm−1 and 3336 cm−1 correspond to stretching vibration of O−H/N − H. Sharp peaks associated with amide linkage (–CONH-) at 1551 cm−1 and 1627 cm−1 strongly prove the dehydration condensation of OPD and DPA during the formation of CDs. The characteristic absorption peaks at 1360 cm−1, 1500 cm−1, 2605 cm−1 and 2903 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of C-N, CC, CO and C-H, respectively. Similarly, XPS spectra are also obtained to prove the successful preparation of CDs from Fig. 1c to f. The wide-scan XPS spectrum in Fig. 1c verifies that CDs possess C, N, and O elements according to the positions of 281.6 eV, 396.8 eV and 529 eV. Simultaneously, their elements contents are listed with C (63.75%), N (14.90%) and O (21.35%) in Table S1. The C1s spectrum confirms the presence of three types of C including -COOH (288.8 eV), C-C/CC (284.8 eV), C-N/CN (285.8 eV) in Fig. 1d. As for the N1s spectrum of CDs in Fig. 1e, the binding energies of pyridine N and pyrrole N with the atomic proportion ability respectively are acquired at 399.4 eV and 400.6 eV. It is worth mentioning that the binding energy of O1s spectrum mainly includes the CO peak at 532.6 eV and the C-OH/C-O-C peak at 531.4 eV in Fig. 1f.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorbance and emission properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To gain deeper insight into the absorbance and emission properties of the CDs, the UV–visible absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra (FL) are investigated. In Fig. S2a, a shoulder peak at 275 nm is ascribed to the π-π * transition of CC among CDs, while a distinctive absorption at 415 nm is assigned to n-π * of C-O/C-N. As depicted in Fig. S2b, the various excitation wavelengths of CDs are measured in the FL spectra. Combined with Fig. S2c, it is firmly confirmed that the best emission center is at 570 nm when the excitation wavelength is set to 420 nm. To better consider the fluorescence performance of CDs, the quantum yield of CDs is 26.13%, employing Rhodamine B (methanol as solvent; QY = 69%) as a reference in Table S2. Compared with the previous CDs designed with choosing similar precursor of OPD [34], the introduction of DPA offers more pyridine rings and carboxyl groups, which is beneficial for enhancing more nitrogen content and forming larger conjugated structures, thus notably enhancing the QY of CDs. Additionally, CDs display an average fluorescence lifetime of 1.98 ns as shown in Fig. S2d.

To broaden the application range of CDs under various conditions, the ionic strength and long-term storage time are determined to assess the optical stability of CDs. As shown in Fig. S3, an ultra-high concentration of NaCl solution (0.5 M) has little effect on the absorbance and fluorescence intensity of CDs, indicating a strong resistance to ion interference. Additionally, the long-term stability of CDs has been investigated under UV irradiation time, temperature, and storage days in Figs. S4 and S5. The absorbance and fluorescence intensity of CDs in Figs. S4a and S4b is almost 100% under the continuous UV lamps (365 nm) irradiation within 480 min, indicating a good anti-photobleaching activity. No obvious absorption and fluorescence variation of CDs is observed in the temperature range from 0 to 60 ◦C in Figs. S4c and S4d, verifying the remarkable temperature-independent optical performance. To verify the long-term stabilities of CDs at different temperatures, Fig. S5a and 5b investigate the absorbance and fluorescence spectra within 20 days at 0 ◦C and 40 ◦C, respectively, which displays no discernible effect. Surprisingly, after storage for 3 months at 5 ◦C, absorbance and emission intensities of CDs are almost unchanged compared with the initial signals in Figs. S5c and S5d, further indicating the outstanding long-term stability of CDs. These findings illustrate that CDs exhibit excellent and long-term stability under harsh conditions.

Combination of the abundant electron-donating groups like -OH and -NH2 groups and electron-withdrawing groups like -COOH and pyridine groups on the surface of CDs, offers the desirable effects for protonating and generating hydrogen bonds, which have the attractions to facilitate intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) and aggregation-induced emission effects (AIE) [35,36].

To gain a deeper insight into the AIE performance, the emission behaviors have been analyzed in water-glycerin and water- N. N-dimethylformamide (DMF) systems. In Fig. 2 a and b, the fluorescence intensities are increased significantly with the gradual increases of glycerin contents (fg), and the maximum emission intensities at 99% fg are approximately 6.81-fold greater than at 0% fg, verifying the high viscosity can restrict intramolecular motions (RIM) and suppress the nonradiative decay channels for enhancing fluorescence emission. Therefore, the RIM process is favorable for the AIE effect [37,38]. Fig. 2c also displays the enhanced fluorescence with the increases of water contents (fw). This characteristic can be explained by the synergistic mechanism of ICT and AIE effect. Compared with polar solvent DMF, the groups of carboxyl, hydroxyl and amino in CDs are more favorable for forming hydrogen bonds with non-polar solvent water, which can effectively restrain the ICT process of donor to receptor transfer and significantly enhance fluorescence [39]. Moreover, the increased rigidity after the formation of hydrogen bonds promotes the aggregation of CDs, further reducing the loss of rotational and vibrational energy of CDs. This process acquires αAIE (I/I0) greater than 9 for enhancing fluorescence of CDs in Fig. 2d, which is consistent with typical AIE characteristics [40]. TEM analysis in Fig. S6 further confirms that CDs forms the aggregated state and exhibits a hydrated particle size of 5.45 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric and fluorescence sensing of pH
ce:section.ce:para: As the pH values change from 3.0 to 11.0, the absorbance and fluorescence spectra of CDs are evaluated in Figs. S7. Interestingly, CDs exhibit strong pH-dependent colorimetric and fluorescence behaviors. As the pH from 3.0 to 7.0, the absorbances of CDs in Figs. S7a and S7b exhibit sharp decrease with blue shifts. Conversely, the emission intensities dramatically increase with red shifts in Fig. S7c and S7d, and the pKa values are determined as 3.95 and 5.83. Notably, CDs demonstrate only slight variation from pH 7.0 to pH 11.0, indicating they are not sensitive under alkaline conditions. Consequently, the designed CDs can function as potential dual-mode sensors, capable of monitoring pH both in both colorimetric and fluorescence modes.

The pH-responsive performance of the constructed dual-mode sensor is evaluated in detail. As demonstrated in Fig. 3a and b, the absorbances of CDs exhibit a noticeable drop as the pH values increase from 3.0 to 5.6, with a good linear relationship described by y = −0.08165x + 1.04571 (R2 =0.9943). Furthermore, the fluorescence intensities of CDs present a sharp increase in the range of pH 3.2–6.8, described by the linear equation of y = 2084.19x − 5806.72 (R2 =0.9975) in Fig. 3c and d. Moreover, Table S3 presents a comparison between proposed sensor and other previously reported pH sensors. The dual-mode colorimetric and fluorescence sensing of CDs can supply more accurate and reliable detection of results than other sensors, making it preferable for bioimaging applications in complex systems. Most importantly, compared with other reported pH-responsive sensors in alkaline systems, as-designed CDs sensor is more conducive to realize biological monitoring, such as distinguishing between cancer cells from normal cells.

Encouragingly, as depicted in Fig. 4a and b, pH-responsive behaviors present outstanding reversibility among CDs. The pH values are regulated by introducing HCl or NaOH, and absorbances of CDs can be repeated six times between pH 3.0 and pH 5.6. A similar switching operation can be realized in fluorescence spectra in the range of pH 3.2 to 6.8. This outstanding reversibility of the absorbance and fluorescence intensity at various pH levels confirms their suitability for repeatedly monitoring pH changes in actual samples. Because of the complexity of the actual environment, the selectivity of CDs to pH is evaluated. No significant absorbance and emission changes after adding common ions and biomolecules in different pHs can be presented in Fig. 4c and d, verifying CDs possess excellent stability and specific responses towards pH sensing.

In the view of response of CDs to pH, such processes may be accordance with intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) and aggregation-induced emission effect (AIE). The systematic investigations are conducted to clarify the response mechanism. When the pH changes from acidic to neutral, the absorbances of CDs decrease and exhibit blue shifts, while the emission intensities increase and demonstrate red shifts. This can be confirmed by molecular orbital theory and the energy levels of CDs are evaluated by the oxidation-reduction scans in Fig. 5. The reduction (Ered) and oxidation (Eox) peaks are explored in − 2.7880 V and 0.1056 V at pH 3.0 in Fig. 5a. Accordingly, the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy levels of CDs in Fig. 5b are calculated at − 1.9520 eV and − 4.8456 eV on the basis of formula ELUMO = -e (Ered +4.4 +0.34), EHOMO = -e (Eox +4.4 +0.34) [41]. The HOMO orbitals of pH 3.0 may be mainly localized on the free phenyl rings, while the LUMO orbitals may be contributed via protonated carboxylates and pyridine nitrogen. These results demonstrate a strong ICT character of CDs at pH 3.0. By contrast, the estimated HOMO and LUMO values with − 2.4344 eV and − 5.4140 eV can also be obtained at pH 7.0, which may be located on the phenyl-based units, preventing the ICT process. Notably, the energy gap between HOMO and LUMO of pH 3.0 is narrower than that of pH 7.0. Therefore, the inhibited ICT process and increased energy gap from pH 3.0 to pH 7.0 are consistent with the absorbance of decrease and blue shifts.

For CDs with emission intensities, with the restriction of ICT, the free molecular motions are inhibited to cause fluorescence enhancement and the red shifts of emission peak from 3.0 to 7.0. Moreover, there is also an AIE effect during the pH transition. This can be further explained by some measurements of TEM, DLS and FTIR spectra. As depicted in Fig. S8, particle sizes in the TEM of CDs are 1.88 nm and 6.27 nm at pH 3.0 and 7.0, respectively, corresponding to the hydrodynamic sizes of 1.931 nm (PDI: 0.236) and 8.971 nm (PDI: 0.290) in Fig. S9. This indicate that the aggregation of CDs realize the fluorescence enhancement with increasing alkalinity. Intriguingly, the stretching vibrations intensities of N-H/O-H decrease from pH 3.0 to pH 7.0 in the FTIR spectra of Fig. S10a, confirming the generation of the intermolecular hydrogen bonding. This discovery can stabilize the ICT process and increase the rigidity of CDs, further reducing rotational and vibrational energy loss and enhancing fluorescence with red shifts. In addition, the surface states are testified by zeta potentials among CDs in Fig. S10b. CDs have a high number of carboxylic, hydroxyl and pyridine groups at a pH of 3.0, CDs readily undergo protonation and have a zeta potential of + 4.32 mV. As the pH increases, the zeta potential becomes more negative, reaching − 13.9 mV. The variations verify that protonation abilities gradually weaken and the electron abilities enhanced from pH 3.0 to 7.0.

Surprisingly, the fluorescence quantum yield at pH 3.0 is only 7.04%, while the quantum yield significantly increases to 83.48% at pH 7.0 (using Rhodamine B as the standard, the quantum yield in methanol is 69.00%) in Table S2. Therefore, the combination of AIE and ICT effect within CDs in Fig. 5c, not only effectively achieve the unique photophysical phenomenon of dual colorimetric and fluorescence effect, but also promote the fluorescence quantum fields, which is also helpful for the multifunctional fluorescent materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric and fluorescence sensing of histamine
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the inherent alkalinity of histamine, pH-responsive CDs can function as the colorimetric and fluorescence sensor for sensitively monitoring histamine. To ensure highly effective detection conditions, the conduct of detailed experiments covering response time and the stability before and after adding histamine, is very crucial. As displayed in Figs. S11 and S12, the response time of 120 s and the stable sensing systems within 14 days demonstrate that CDs hold rapid-response abilities and fantastic long-term stability for determining histamine. In Fig. 6a, the absorbance of CDs at 448 nm gradually decreases after introducing 30–1000 µM of histamine. The absorbance and histamine concentration exhibit an excellent linear relationship described by y = −1.1244 * 10−4x + 0.57137 (R2 =0.9932) with a low limit of detection (LOD) of 10.11 µM as presented in Fig. 6b. Moreover, the emission intensities are also obtained in the histamine range of 25–1000 µM with a great linear equation of y = 2.4537x+ 796.02 (R2 =0.9978), and the LOD is determined to be 6.96 µM (S/N = 3) in Fig. 6d and e.

The selectivity of the constructed sensing platform is evaluated, as shown in Fig. 6c and f. Some biogenic amines and amino acids present similar structure and performances to histamine, which may interfere with histamine monitoring in actual food samples. Ethylenediamine (EDA), five biogenic amines (spermidine, tryptamine, cadaverine, putrescine and spermine), three alkaline amino acids (arginine, lysine and histidine), ammonia and methylamine are representative interferents that are widely present in practical samples. They are determined in uniform systems and dispersed in fixed CD solutions. The developed system exhibits a satisfactory level of specificity towards histamine, as evidenced by the absorbance and fluorescence changes occurring exclusively in the presence of histamine. Minor changes are observed when other analytes are added at higher concentrations, indicating that only histamine could react specifically with CDs. Surprisingly, a corresponding sensor with other reported sensing platforms for detecting histamine are concluded in Table S4, the designed CDs have the characteristics of multi-mode sensing of histamine, achieving higher sensitivity and wider linearity monitoring and avoiding the harm caused by experimental errors such as instrument and human factors.

The sensing mechanism of histamine is exploited in Figs. S13 and S14. The protonation of the aliphatic amino group promotes the alkalization of aqueous histamine solutions (pKa = 9.8) [42], and histamine can cause pH-responsive CDs absorbance and fluorescence intensity variations. In Fig. S13, the absorbance is decreased by 26.63% with slight blue shifts, and the fluorescence intensity exhibits an increase of 2.03 times accompanied by red shifts among CDs. This is consistent with the pH-responsive behavior of CDs, indicating that the response to histamine can also be explained by AIE and ICT mechanisms. Besides, the π–π interaction between the pyridine, benzene ring of CDs, and the imidazole ring of histamine promotes them closer, efficiently suppressing the rotation of CDs and triggering AIE effect, thus resulting in the increase and red shifts of fluorescence [43]. In addition, hydrogen bonds are formed between the H atoms of the amino group of histamine and the O atoms of the carboxyl group of CDs. Such interaction can block the transfer of charge from donor to acceptor, further inhibiting the ICT process and enhancing the fluorescence. Notably, hydrogen bonding interactions or π–π stacking may lead to a decrease in absorption and a blue shift due to the formation of compact structure. Above all, combined with pH variations, π–π interactions and hydrogen bonding interactions, the fantastic colorimetric and fluorescence dual-mode sensor as well as visual sensing of histamine is realized, as shown in Fig. S14.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone imaging detection of pH and histamine
ce:section.ce:para: Interestingly, the colors of CDs dramatically vary with increasing pH and histamine under sunlight and ultraviolet lamps (UV lamps) in Fig. 7, so colorimetry and fluorimetry imaging based on the smartphone are built for portable and visual sensing at different pH values and histamine concentrations. Compared to the visual recognition by naked-eyes, smartphones can immediately capture many related images and read R, G and B values, which is highly beneficial and more sensitive for identifying color variations. In Fig. 7a, with increasing pH from 3.0 to 7.0, the visible colors of CDs change from yellow to light yellow under sunlight with a good linearity between R/(R+G+B) and pH values (y = −0.0207x+0.5263, R2 =0.9947). Fig. 7b illustrates that the fluorescence colors under UV lamps gradually enhance from pH 3.0 to 5.8 with a linear relationship described by y = 0.06947x+ 0.04648 (R2 =0.9928). Simultaneously, increased histamine concentrations also achieve the obvious color variations. Fig. 7c illustrates the significant yellow attenuation under sunlight upon adding histamine from 50 to 1200 µM, and the linearity can be observed with the equation y = 5.3385 * 10−5x + 0.41708 (R2 = 0.9921), with an LOD up to 18.87 µM. Fig. 7d shows the process of color transformation under UV lamps, with the brightness increasing as the histamine concentration rises from 50 to 1000 µM. The equation is y = 3.91542 * 10−5x + 0.33499 (R2 =0.9945) with the LOD of 18.87 µM. These results strongly confirm that CDs integrated smartphones are promising sensing systems for on-site and real-time monitoring of pH and histamine via apparent color changes.

To avoid interference from different models of smartphones on the detection results, five different models of common mobile phones (iPhone 15, Xiaomi 14, Honor 90, Huawei Nova and Vivo S17) are used to capture and compare the changed colors with OPPO Reno SE 5 G under sunlight in Fig. S15. The phenomena verify that the brightness R of the samples and background signals captured by different smartphones is not entirely the same, but their ratio of brightness R and background signal (R+G+B) shows a synchronous increasing or decreasing trend. Therefore, we use the ratio of the changing brightness R to the background signal (R+G+B) as the vertical axis to construct linear relationships with pH in different smartphones, which can effectively overcome interference in imaging quality caused by different smartphone models and enhance accuracy of detection results. The unknown pH of CDs is detected by taking photos with different smartphones and substituting R/(R+G+B) into the different linear relationships. As shown in Table S5, the analysis shows that the obtained pH values are the same approximately, further proving that multiple different types of smartphones have certain accuracy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The stability of CDs for pH and histamine sensing
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the optical stability of CDs for pH and histamine sensing, the continuous irradiation, temperature and long-time storage are determined. As shown in Fig. S16, CDs have super optical stability under the continuous ultra-violet (365 nm) irradiation for 4 h in the presence of various pH values and histamine contents. No distinct variation of CDs is observed during the detection of pH and histamine in the temperature range from 0 to 80 ◦C in Fig. S17. More importantly, the optical intensities of CDs at different pH and histamine concentrations display minor fluctuations at 0 ◦C and 40 ◦C within 0–20 days in Fig. S18, manifesting that CDs is stable during the long-term sensing of pH and histamine, and are expected to be acted as an ideal sensor for tracking pH and histamine levels in real samples over time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell imaging of pH and histamine
ce:section.ce:para: The exceptional biocompatibility of the fabricated CDs has been utilized for subsequent biological applications. Consequently, cytotoxicity studies of CDs are conducted on HeLa cells at various doses. In Fig. S19, the high cell viabilities of 90% at 100 μg mL−1 and 80% at 400 μg mL−1 prove minor cytotoxicity of CDs. .

Figure 12 illustrates that weak fluorescence emission enhances from pH 3.0 to 7.0 in the red channel of HeLa cells after incubating with CDs for 10 min. Furthermore, in Fig. S20a, HeLa cells incubated with CDs exhibit weak emission, and the subsequent introduction of histamine (200 μM) leads to an apparent fluorescence increase within 10 min, verifying the capability of the as-synthesized CDs to determine histamine contents in living cells. These results indicate that CDs can efficiently serve as the sensor for real-time and visual monitoring of intracellular pH and histamine fluctuations.

To demonstrate the feasibility of CDs in distinguishing cancer cells from normal cells, cancer cells (HeLa and MCF-7) and normal cells (HL7702) are treated with CDs for 10 min, as shown in Fig. S20b. This illustrates the weak fluorescence in the red channels of HeLa and MCF-7 cells, while relatively bright fluorescence signals are observed in HL7702 cells. This observation is consistent with the theory that cancer cells exhibit stronger acidity than normal cells. This suggests CDs can differentiate cancer from normal cells by detecting pH fluctuations.

To cultivate its potential for practical food samples, CDs are combined with test strip paper to develop a portable and visual platform for histamine. The strip-embedded CDs are fixed to the same position, and the smartphone is locked at a certain height and angle to acquire images and extract R, G, B color spaces. The strips loaded with CDs maintain their original colors for 10 days, verifying their superior stability due to the excellent physical and optical performance of CDs, as displayed in Fig. S21. Interestingly, the strips exhibit noticeable color changes under both sunlight and UV irradiation with increasing concentrations of histamine.

Additionally, raw fish samples are monitored to assess their freshness. The monitoring system examines the dynamic freshness variations of fish samples over a period of 120 h by holding them at four different temperatures (−21, 0, 25, and 35 °C), as shown in Fig. 9. The colors turn to light yellow in the sunlight and transform to bright fluorescence under the UV lamps at 35 °C for 12 h and 25 °C for 24 h, respectively, thereby implying that histamine displays rapid production in fish samples at higher temperatures. When fish samples are stored at 0 °C, they maintain freshness for approximately 72 h. The fish retains great freshness for a longer time at − 21 °C than at other relatively high temperatures. These colors turn to shadows in the sunlight and change to bright yellow fluorescence on the fifth day.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practicability of histamine detection in samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further assess practicability, the histamine content in seafood specimens is determined using CDs-based colorimetric and fluorescence sensor, and subsequent test outcomes are compared with the Chinese standard method (GB5009.208—2016). Measurements are performed six times, and the histamine content in raw fish, raw shrimp, and shellfish is detected using a formula developed by the Chinese standard method. The detection is performed under various experimental settings and the results are presented. Histamine concentrations for different types of substances after being placed at 35 °C for 0.5 and 3 days are presented in Table S6, the comparison between the calculated contents, based on absorbance and fluorescence intensity, and the results obtained from the Chinese standard method, demonstrates that the calculated contents, based on absorbance and fluorescence intensity, exhibit a significant level of precision. Meanwhile, using the smartphone-mediated colorimetric and fluorescence method, the contents of histamine are sequentially detected for different days (0–5 days) at the same temperature of 25 °C as depicted in Table S7. Then the raw fish samples are also tested after three days at different temperatures (−21 °C, 0 °C, 25 °C, 35 °C), as shown in Table S8. Histamine content is also determined using the Chinese standard method under the same conditions during sensing process, which are in basic accordance with the determinations of smartphone imaging. The aforementioned results confirm that optical dual-mode CDs-based sensor can measure histamine concentrations with high accuracy in complex food samples, as well as simplicity and good sensitivity.

The designed CDs-based colorimetric and fluorescence approach not only exhibits the pH-responsive ability with excellent reversibility and selectivity, but also sensitively determines histamine with wide linearity up to 1200 µM, short response time (120 s) and long-term stability. Compared with previously reported optical sensors, the significant superiorities of CDs including the following: (1) the dual-mode sensor with facile synthesis and cheap raw materials is ingeniously designed, which only requires one-step preparation without the assistance of complicated synthesis procedures. (2) the dual-signals introduction of colorimetic and fluorescence can rapidly and accurately recognize pH and histamine, improving the reliability of detection results; (3) In comparison with some semi-quantitative measurement of pH and histamine using the naked eye identification, integrating smartphone and sensor system is more convenient for achieving the quantitative determination of pH and histamine without complexed equipments. (4) Sensitive and rapid visualization of pH and histamine can simultaneously bridge biological and food monitoring due to favorable optical property, low cytotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility and long-term stability of CDs. (5) This work is expected to provide the ideal reference for constructing smartphone-integrated sensor with dual-mode signals, promoting the rapid development of biological and environmental fields.

A smart multifunctional CD-based sensor is successfully developed using a simple solvothermal method. The as-prepared CDs enable dual-signal sensing of pH and histamine via colorimetric and fluorescence variations. During the sensing process, from acidic to alkaline conditions, the absorbance of CDs decreases, whereas the fluorescence intensity increases. This is due to AIE and ICT characters. Inspired by increased pH after adding histamine, CDs exhibit a sensitive response to histamine through colorimetric and fluorescence methods. The CDs exhibit exceptional linearities of 30–1000 µM and 25–1000 µM, respectively. Consequently, the designed nano-sensor, which possesses exceptionally high biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity, is effectively employed for intracellular pH imaging to differentiate between normal and malignant cells, thereby demonstrating promising prospects for biological analysis. Additionally, fast and sensitive analysis of histamine for food freshness evaluation is achieved using CDs as indicators of food safety. More importantly, the utilization of CDs-mediated smartphone and strip applications provide convenient and exceptionally precise ways for visually monitoring pH and histamine levels using diverse colors. Therefore, the as-prepared CDs could serve as a bridge for connecting biological imaging and food monitoring, providing a meaningful guideline for the subsequent construction of CDs with colorimetric and fluorescent modes, which would have applications in the environmental and biosensing fields.

High amounts of histamine, hazardous substances produced by spoiled food, pose adverse effects for food poisoning and human health including vomiting, head-aches and death. Based on their harmfulness, the threshold histamine should be strictly controlled. So rapid and accurate histamine evaluation is crucial. This work employs a colorimetric and fluorescent CDs for pH and histamine dual-mode monitoring, achieving dynamic bridges of hazardous histamine between biological and food monitoring. Interestingly, sensitive color changes make smartphone-integrated sensor more portable and visual than other probes. This enables CDs a multifunctional biosensor for monitoring histamines with realistically detecting ranges to meet practical needs.

Xincheng Sun: Conceptualization, Supervision. Xiaoran Zhang: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Elias Hasan: Formal analysis. Jing Wang: Data curation. Chuan Dong: Funding acquisition, Project administration. Wenjing Lu: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Shaomin Shuang: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Ayesha Aziz: Writing – review & editing. Muhammad Asif: Methodology, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22274090) and Shanxi Province Graduate Student Education Innovation Project (2023KY022).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2024.134271.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
